So, talking about now Redshift from Amazon.
So the background you understand is that there is a long history of people, of companies
and open source projects trying to make distributed versions of Postgres.
Again, Postgres came out in the 1980s at a Berkeley.
It was traditionally a single node system.
In the 1990s, there was a commercial version of Postgres called Alastra, which is a completely
separate code base as far as I know.
And the original academic version of Postgres didn't support SQL, it supported Quell.
And then some Berkeley grad students took the old academic version of Postgres, add a
SQL to support to it.
That's why it's called Postgres QL, is the official name.
And then as the 2000s came along, people were looking, you know, trying to make it distributed.
And so there's a lot of attempts to do this, and the OTP side is a side of things.
There's Postgres XC, StormDB, which was based on Postgres XC, but then they went under
or they got bought by Translattis and Translattis had their own thing.
Postgres XL was maybe the one that what people are most excited about.
And that hasn't been updated on anything since 2015, 2016 or something.
All right.
So that project basically has died out.
Now there's systems like UGabyte for example that took the front end of Postgres, then ripped
out the bottom half and made that distributed.
But it's not exactly the same.
If you were trying to do this, you know, natively inside of Postgres.
And then there's a lot of systems that were doing this for OLAB as well.
Again, the mid 2000s went to sort of a SOCOP.
